rulings.Case Title: JOHN F. COX JR. ET AL VS. FCA US LLC., A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANTS FCA US LLC, MICHAEL STEAD'S AUTO DEPOT, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Defendants FCA US LLC and Michael Stead's Auto Depot, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication of plaintiff John Cox, Jr.'s request for Civil Code 3345 treble penalties is granted. Mr. Cox's supplemental interrogatory responses dated April 2, 2018 show that Mr. Cox does not now have and is unable to obtain any evidence that: 1) at the time Mr. Cox made his request to buyback his 2011 Jeep Grand Cherokee, either defendant knew or should have known his age and 2) defendants' alleged wrongful refusal to buyback the Jeep caused Mr. Cox to suffer any of the losses identified in Civil Code 3345(b)(2). Based on those responses, defendants satisfied their summary adjudication burden. Because Mr. Cox failed to identify any facts creating a triable dispute on either of these issues, defendants are entitled to summary adjudication in their favor on Mr. Cox's request for Civil Code 3345 treble penalties. Mr. Cox presented no evidence that either defendant knew of his age when made his buyback request. Mr. Cox's appearance and the fact that he was retired are insufficient bases to infer that he was a senior citizen at the time he made his buyback request. Nor has Mr. Cox provided any evidence that there was any reason why defendants could or should have reviewed any references to Mr. Cox's age in documentation received by them at or around the time of Mr. Cox's purchase of the Jeep when several years later he made his buyback request and did not make any mention of his age. Moreover, Mr. Cox's purchase of the Jeep when he was not a "senior citizen" does not constitute a section 3345(b)(2) loss. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: TERRY SCHENK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied. There is a triable dispute whether the conduct of the bus operator, for which the City is vicariously liable, fell below the heightened standard of care applicable to common carriers per CCP 2100. A reasonable juror could find that the driver failed to "to do all that human care, vigilance, and foresight reasonably can do under the circumstances" (Lopez v. Southern Cal. Rapid Transit Dist. (1985) 40 Cal.3d 780, 785) based on evidence that Mr. Celeste did not see Mr. Schenk nor the other people holding the bar and did not warn Mr. Shenk using the AP system to move his hand away from the bar despite his training to use his mirrors and nothing was blocking his view in the mirrors. There is evidence that Mr. Celeste was trained to use his mirrors to check the interior of the bus before opening the door, not to open the door if he sees a passenger blocking the door, and to alert a passenger to move his limbs away from the door before opening the door. Because Mr. Schenk defeats this motion based on a theory that the City is vicariously liable for the conduct of the bus operator, there is no need to address the issue of the viability of any dangerous condition theory asserted by Mr. Schenk. rulings.Case Title: JULIA SCHAEFER ET AL VS. CALIFORNIA AND PIERCE APARTMENTS, LP ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Financial Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF JULIA SCHAEFER, MICHAEL MIZUNO, HALEY NUSBAUM, ERIC NUSBAUM's Motion For Financial Discovery. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion for pre-trial discovery of defendants financial condition is denied. Plaintiffs have not sufficiently established a substantial probability of prevailing on their claim for punitive damages. See Civ. Code, § 3295(c); Jabro v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 754. The court has weighed the evidence presented by both sides and does not find there is a strong likelihood plaintiffs will prevail on their punitive damages claim. Guardado v. Superior Court (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 91. rulings.Case Title: LVOVICH & SZUCSKO, P.C. A CALIFORNIA PROFESSIONAL VS. YURI GALASHOV ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 6, PLAINTIFF LVOVICH & SZUCSKO, P.C.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Plaintiff Lvovich & Szucsko, P.C.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. L&S has failed to satisfy its summary judgment burden that it is entitled to $584,002.02 on either or both of its breach of contract and common counts claims. Both claims are premised on the existence and enforceability of the April 30, 2012 Attorney-Client Fee Agreement. However, some of the fees L&S seeks on this motion were for services purportedly rendered before the date of that agreement and there is no evidence submitted by L&S that the agreement applies to those fees. Moreover, the agreement provides for a deed of trust in favor of L&S to be recorded which requires that L&S comply with the requirements of Rule 3-300 of the Rules of Professional Conduct and L&S has not submitted any evidence that it complied with all of those requirements. In particular, there is no evidence that the contract term regarding the deed of trust was fair and reasonable to Mr. Galashov or that the explanation of that term was disclosed to him in a manner which reasonably should have been understood by him. Furthermore, while Mr. Galashov apparently put his initials in the portion of the contract stating that he was given a reasonable opportunity to consult an independent lawyer, there is no evidence that he was given such an opportunity and the fact that the contract is both dated and signed the same day suggests that he might not have been given such an opportunity. Even if L&S did satisfy its summary judgment burden, Mr. Galsahov's declaration creates triable disputes regarding L&S' entitlement to fees for services purportedly rendered by it before the date of the April 30, 2012 contract and whether L&S complied with the requirements of Rule 3-300. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JASON MERTZ-PRICKETT VS. CHRISTOPHER BRUNO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 7, PLAINTIFF JASON MERTZ-PRICKETT'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint. Plaintiff Jason Mertz-Prickett's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is granted. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings at any stage in a case. and none of the defendants have identified any legal prejudice to them if the motion is granted. Even if Mr. Mertz-Prickett was not as diligent in seeking to amend as he might have been, no defendant has identified any legal prejudice to him or it if the motion is granted. Because the amended complaint seeks to add two paragraphs to an existing cause of action and not an entirely new cause of action, the argument that the proposed amendment doesn't state a valid claim lacks merit and, in all events, defendants are not foreclosed from filing a demurrer to the IIED claim, although doing so is highly unlikely to be efficacious. rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH FOX VS. NORMA R. TORRES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANTS ANTHONY COURTNEY, ANTHONY RATTONETTI, PURIN PHANICHPHANT, 293-295-297 CHURCH STREET AND 1992 15TH STREET'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendants Antony Courtney, Anthony Rattonetti, Purin Phanichphant, and 293-295-297 Church Street and 1992 15th Street Homeowner Association's motion for summary judgment is denied. The HOA withdrew its motion in the reply memorandum. Mr. Courtney, Mr. Rattonetti, and Mr. Phanichphant are not entitled to summary judgment in their favor based on their Civil Code 580 affirmative defense. Section 580 does not preclude liability against the individual moving parties based on evidence that, when they were officers of the HOA, they failed to conduct an inspection of the balcony and guardrail, which all parties acknowledge was part of the common area, which led to plaintiff Joseph Fox's injuries. The HOA had a duty to repair that common area per Civil Code 2775(a)(3) and thus a duty to inspect that area per McNally v. Ward (1961) 192 Cal.App.2d 871, 875. Per Frances T. v. Village Green Owners Assn. (1986) 42 Cal.3d 490, 507-508, an officer of a homeowners' association can be liable for his participation in the association's failure to perform a duty imposed on the association. The individual moving parties' liability for failure to inspect the common area which led to Mr. Fox's injuries does not arise solely by reason of the individual moving defendants' ownership interest in the common area, but also from their alleged negligent failure to take action as officers of the HOA. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Fox is required to prepare a proposed order that complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: RAPHAEL STRICKER VS. BARBARA BUCHMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Special Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Complaint Under Anti-Slapp Statute
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Monday, August 13, 2018, Line 15, DEFENDANTS BARBARA BUCHMAN, INTERNATIONAL LYME AND ASSOCIATED DISEASES SOCIETY'S Notice Of Motion And Special Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Complaint Under Anti-Slapp Statute. Defendants Barbara Buchman and International Lyme and Associated Diseases Society's anti-SLAPP motion to strike the second and third causes of action for declaratory relief and defamation in plaintiff Raphael Stricker's complaint is denied. Assuming without deciding that defendants satisfied their first prong burden that the declaratory relief and defamation claims arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute, Dr. Stricker has shown that there is minimal merit to both of the claims. Dr. Stricker's declaration presents sufficient evidence to show that, per the reverse summary judgment procedure that governs the second prong, there is a triable dispute whether defendants acted with malice to defeat their affirmative defense of the common interest privilege. Dr. Stricker's declaration shows that defendants were aware that the article that he sought to have published by Dove Press and Chronic Disease International was his original unplagiarized work yet they knowingly stated otherwise and took actions to prevent the publication of the article as well as removed Dr. Stricker from the Paris conference. This conduct, if believed to have occurred, shows that defendants acted in reckless disregard of Dr. Striker's rights and also tends to show that defendants' actions were motivated by ill will towards Dr. Stricker. Dr. Stricker's request for attorney's fees is denied because this motion is neither frivolous nor solely intended to cause delay. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Dr. Stricker is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)
